{"society_power": 8.5, "state_power": 6.0, "society_change": 0.5, "state_change": -0.5, "key_event": "Sectional crisis over slavery; Bleeding Kansas and Dred Scott", "reasoning": "Explosive growth of the abolitionist/free-soil press, party realignment, and mass mobilization (Uncle Tom's Cabin, Republican rise) strengthened organized civil society, while Bleeding Kansas, the caning of Sumner, and eroding legitimacy after Kansas-Nebraska and Dred Scott showed the federal state's declining ability to monopolize violence and command shared legitimacy. Despite expanded coercive reach (Fugitive Slave Act) and administrative growth via railroads, net state cohesion weakened amid sectional fracture."}
